critique.summary: The article discusses a study by the University at Albany that finds repeated exposure to cybersecurity requirements can lead to 'security fatigue,' causing employees to become emotionally exhausted and disengaged from security practices. The research emphasizes the importance of organizational support and simplified processes to maintain employee compliance without overwhelming them.
mainPoints: Repeated cybersecurity demands can cause 'security fatigue,' leading to emotional exhaustion and disengagement.
Employees often become overwhelmed by complex security tasks, which can result in weaker security behaviors like reusing passwords or ignoring warnings.
Providing training, simplifying security processes, and organizational support can help reduce fatigue and improve compliance.
The study surveyed nearly 300 U.S. employees and highlights the need for sustainable security systems that do not overburden staff.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reports the findings of the study published in the European Journal of Information Systems, supported by quotes from the lead researcher Sanjay Goel. It references a credible academic source and provides a balanced perspective on the human side of cybersecurity, aligning with existing research on security fatigue and employee behavior.
considerations: The article does not specify the specific methodologies used in the study or how representative the sample of 300 employees is of the broader workforce.
It does not discuss potential differences across industries or organizational sizes that might affect security fatigue.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the increasing complexity and fatigue caused by security measures in digital systems, highlighting issues like permission overload, compliance disconnect, and user frustration. Several comments suggest structural security and minimal user intervention as potential solutions.

comments.author: onetimeusename
comments.text: I think security became part of compliance so security recommendations got detached from actual security. It seems like a lot of security recommendations are just busy work that justifies having a huge compliance industry. So an example of this might be security scanners for code where the output is not even useful. But using the tool, which searches for irrelevant findings, is required for compliance even if it basically does nothing for security.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully critiques the disconnect between compliance requirements and real security needs, highlighting a common industry issue.

comments.author: compiler-guy
comments.text:
I have seen this phenomenon especially at a couple of FAANGs over the past couple of years. Things are getting locked down so much, and so many special permissions are required that now people ask for permissions to systems or procedures preemptively. Because by the time they know if they will need it or not, it's too late.

And no one in the security business seems to consider the overall burden of yet another step. Each of which is simple in by itself, but cumulatively they are a giant hassle, and so people look for workarounds.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes how excessive restrictions lead to workarounds, undermining security and increasing user frustration.

comments.author: dijit
comments.text:
thats part of why NIST updated their password rotation recommendations from 90 days to indefinite: people pay lip service to security if it is too inconvenient. you have to try to meet people where they are.

Preaching is not a strong motivator for long.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly references NIST's guidance change, emphasizing how inconvenience impacts security practices.

comments.author: donatj
comments.text: The level of lockdown in current years is wild. With our 2FA requirements and SSO, signing into GitHub every morning takes me something like eight clicks and a solid minute. Everything has gotten so locked down in recent years, people are working so hard to protect what are largely basic CRUD apps
comments.analysis: The comment effectively illustrates how layered security measures can hinder productivity and user experience.

comments.author: randusername
comments.text:
This is a much bigger problem than just security.

Incidents are inevitable at scale, but risk management at scale is an append-only operation that eventually becomes so complex and suffocating the only recourse is noncompliance.

Even going to the doctor I find myself pleading with the staff to just let me see my PCP instead of going through the full process. It takes 30 minutes now to get through the opening interrogation about overseas travel, human trafficking, vaccine awareness, anxiety and depression panels, domestic violence questions, multi-part questions about recent falls, and everything else that they keep tacking on. Usually in triplicate, waiting room forms, questions from the nurse, questions from the doctor.

And I know behind each of these individual decisions there is a horror story or someone proactively trying to prevent one, but altogether they create their own.
comments.analysis: This comment draws a compelling analogy between security overload and bureaucratic healthcare, highlighting systemic issues.

comments.author: gz5
comments.text: Absolutely. Easier said than done, but the best security is structural security - as near to invisible for end users as possible. This needs to be the goal, imo, even if not fully achievable.
comments.analysis: This comment advocates for unobtrusive security, aligning with best practices for user-friendly security design.

comments.author: kstenerud
comments.text:
And now we're at the threshold of the next level of security fatigue: permission fatigue.

It's shocking how little people are paying attention to this upcoming security nightmare. It wouldn't take much for a bad actor to poison an AI session to wait for you to start selecting yes, yes, yes and then slip in something bad.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies a new facet of security fatigue, emphasizing the risks of user complacency.

comments.author: scuff3d
comments.text:
Was talking with someone about this yesterday. From cold start, for me to get to the VM I do my actual work on I have to

1. Enter a password to decrypt the computer

2. Enter a username and password to log into my account

3. Enter another set of credentials to access the corporate VPN

4. Enter another username and password to access the network the VM is on

5. Enter another username and password to get to the actual machine

6. And then navigate a nest of authorization for docker/git/etc to actually do anything useful
comments.analysis: This detailed account exemplifies how layered security can hinder efficiency and user experience.

comments.author: languagehacker
comments.text: Nice to see SUNY Albany on here!
comments.analysis: While humorous, this comment does not contribute substantively to the discussion of security fatigue.

comments.author: nathan_compton
comments.text: The number of times I have to "single sign on" is truly maddening.
comments.analysis: This comment states a common frustration but offers little new insight into security fatigue.

comments.author: lloydatkinson
comments.text: Who knew bombarding users with 2FA, 3FA, MFA requests to their phone 20 times a day would cause fatigue! /s
comments.analysis: The sarcastic tone diminishes the seriousness of the security fatigue issue, bordering on trolling.

comments.author: general_reveal
comments.text:
Just get off as many of these platform as you can. That’s about the only security that you’ll ever get. If you are still in the Matrix, listen the weirdos on here that take “don’t trust anything” seriously to the point of absurdity.

The Matrix was not fiction. Our modern internet is a system. You have to figure out how to live truly free from it, because it absolutely owns you.

__

Revelation 13:16–17

“And he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads: And that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark…”
comments.analysis: This comment promotes an extreme stance that dismisses practical security measures, leaning toward conspiracy.
